summary: DHH argues that enterprise Java programmers' resistance to Ruby on Rails stems from a fundamental confusion: they conflate the patterns and practices developed within Java (MVC, ORM, TDD, DDD) with the Java language itself. Because these patterns were primarily documented and exemplified in Java, many developers mistakenly believe they're intrinsic to Java or to static typing. DHH contends that Ruby on Rails implements the same core patterns, just reinterpreted for a dynamic language context. He suggests that if developers recognized their attachment is to the practices rather than the language, their view of dynamic languages would change dramatically.
key_points: Java attracted top talent who developed important patterns (MVC, ORM, TDD, DDD), but because these were documented in Java-first contexts, people conflated the patterns with the language
The poor quality of popular PHP applications reinforced the false belief that dynamic languages inherently lack good engineering practices
Ruby on Rails implements largely the same patterns and practices as enterprise Java, just reinterpreted for a dynamic language context
Java programmers' attachment to the JVM is likely an attachment to the patterns and practices, not the language itself
Separating the concept of good software practices from any specific language would dramatically improve perceptions of dynamic languages
money_quotes: There's confusion of where language ends and patterns and practices begin.
Who's to blame someone from thinking that it's all the same? That it's because of, and only in, Java that you would get access to these patterns and practices.
Ruby on Rails (and many other dynamic languages, frameworks, and applications) are largely built with a relevant subset of the same patterns and used following the same practices as Java!
The simplicity afforded in the new context can camouflage the resemblance, but the core ideas (and ideals) are the same.
The attachment many Java programmers have with the language and JVM could very likely be an attachment to the patterns and practices that these technologies are used with instead.

If we can get that idea to take hold — that it's not about Java but about MVC, ORM, frameworks, test- and domain-driven development — I think the view on dynamic languages and frameworks in general, and Ruby on Rails in particular, would change dramatically.
key_insight: Enterprise developers' loyalty to Java is actually loyalty to software patterns and practices that are language-agnostic, and recognizing this distinction is the key to opening minds about dynamic languages like Ruby.
